name: Keshava-Kashmiri Bhatta
dates_notes: Mughal-era; dated by manuscript and reported debates with Caitanya's teacher-line.

lineage_polemical.note: Reported debates with the proto-Gauḍīya line make Keśava-Kāśmīrī a conduit-figure for the Bhedābheda → Acintya-Bhedābheda transition.

core_thesis:
Keśava-Kāśmīrī Bhaṭṭa is the late-medieval consolidator of Nimbārka-line *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*. He works principally as a sub-commentator and as a Bhagavad-Gītā exegete rather than as a doctrinal innovator. The school's core is preserved without alteration: *bheda* and *abheda* are intrinsic and equally primordial; *cit*, *acit*, and *Īśvara* (Kṛṣṇa with Rādhā) are co-eternally real; the *jīva* is eternal *aṃśa* of Brahman; *bhakti* and *prapatti* are the operative path; liberation does not erase the *jīva*'s real distinctness.

The theological signature is a strongly Kṛṣṇa-centred Vaiṣṇavism with developed Rādhā-doctrine. The figure of Rādhā here carries more theological weight than in the *Pārijāta-Saurabha* itself, and several motifs that the Gauḍīya tradition will later systematise — the Vraja-centred presentation of Kṛṣṇa, the structural place of Rādhā as inseparable consort, the centrality of *prapatti* — are already present in workable form.

The reported debate with Caitanya, preserved in Gauḍīya hagiography (notably the *Caitanya-Caritāmṛta*), makes Keśava a conduit-figure between the Nimbārka *bhedābheda* line and the emerging *acintya-bhedābheda* of Bengal. The historical detail of the encounter is irrecoverable; the doctrinal proximity that makes the report intelligible is not. Whether or not the meeting occurred as reported, Keśava's late-Nimbārka theology is the proximate doctrinal context against which Caitanya's *acintya* qualification can be read as a deliberate intervention.

engaged_works.title: Krama-Dipika
engaged_works.summary: The *Krama-Dīpikā* is Keśava-Kāśmīrī's manual of Kṛṣṇa-*upāsanā* and *mantra-rahasya*. Its organising principle is the *krama* — graded sequence — by which the practitioner is incorporated into Kṛṣṇa-with-Rādhā worship through *mantra*, *dhyāna*, *nyāsa*, and structured *prapatti*. Doctrinally the work presupposes *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*. The *jīva*'s eternal status as *aṃśa* of Brahman is the precondition for *bhakti* being a real, mutual, non-collapsing relation rather than a provisional means whose truth is later cancelled. The institutional reach of the text exceeds its sectarian origin: Gauḍīya practitioners cite it, and it is one of the channels through which proto-Caitanya devotional vocabulary acquires a continuous receiving lineage. Its Rādhā-*tattva* in particular prefigures motifs that Rūpa Gosvāmī and Jīva Gosvāmī will later raise to systematic theology — the inseparability of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa as joint object of worship, the structural primacy of the *gopī*-mode, the conception of devotion as relational participation. The *Krama-Dīpikā* is therefore both a Nimbārka practice-manual and a transmission-text for early-modern Vaiṣṇava devotional vocabulary.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Prakashika
engaged_works.summary: The *Tattva-Prakāśikā* is Keśava-Kāśmīrī's commentary on the Bhagavad-Gītā from within Nimbārka *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*. Across the eighteen chapters the construal is consistent: Kṛṣṇa is *parabrahman* identical with the Brahman of the Upaniṣads; the *jīva* is eternally distinct-and-non-distinct *aṃśa*; the *prakṛti–puruṣa* exposition of chapters seven and thirteen licenses the school's three-real ontology rather than any *māyā*-driven reduction. Verses standardly recruited by Advaita to support *jagat-mithyātva* — for example 7.4–7 on the *aparā* and *parā* *prakṛti*s — are re-read so as to preserve world-reality and the *jīva*'s genuine ontological standing. The polemical horizon is twofold: Śaṅkara is engaged for collapsing difference, Bhāskara for treating it as adventitious. Where Śaṅkara is the more frequent target, Bhāskara's *aupādhika* qualification is the closer rival, and Keśava marks the demarcation carefully. The commentary is the principal Nimbārka-line *Gītā* reading and a key channel for the school's wider circulation in early-modern Vaiṣṇavism.

engaged_works.title: Kaustubha-Prabha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on the Vedānta-Kaustubha.
engaged_works.summary: The *Kaustubha-Prabhā* is Keśava-Kāśmīrī's gloss on Śrīnivāsa's *Vedānta-Kaustubha*, and so a sub-sub-commentary on Nimbārka's *Pārijāta-Saurabha*. It is the school's mature scholastic instrument. Where Śrīnivāsa had supplied the *pūrvapakṣa–uttarapakṣa* architecture absent from Nimbārka's terse bhāṣya, Keśava sharpens the technical defence against the post-Śaṅkara Advaitic mainstream of his own period — Vivaraṇa-line and Bhāmatī-line opponents whose apparatus is more developed than the *māyā-vāda* Nimbārka could have addressed in the twelfth century. The contested *adhikaraṇas* are those concerning *māyā*, *jīva*-plurality, and *Brahma-pariṇāma*. The school's signature commitment is preserved at every step: *bheda* and *abheda* are equally intrinsic, neither is reducible to the other, and Bhāskara's *aupādhika* qualification is refused as decisively as the Advaitic reduction of the *jīva* to appearance. The *Kaustubha-Prabhā* is the standard Sanaka-sampradāya reference at the level of fine-grained Vedāntic interpretation and the canonical late-medieval statement of the line.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
sarveṣu varṇeṣu tathāśrameṣu
nārīṣu nānāhvaya-janmabheṣu |
dātā phalānām abhivāñchitānāṃ
drāg eva gopālaka-mantra eṣaḥ ||

pada_analysis.gloss: among those of various names and birth-stars

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: nānā āhvayāni ca nānā janmabhāni ca yeṣu teṣu
samasa_vigrahas.note: The commentary explains this as covering differing names and birth-stars.

karaka_structure.note: sphere within which the mantra gives results
further sphere of applicability
explicit inclusion of women
further extension across differing ritual-birth designations
the subject is expressed through a nominal predication with dātā
genitive relation to dātā: giver of the desired fruits

key_passages.english_close: Among all varṇas, and likewise among the āśramas as well. Among women too. And among those of various names and birth-stars. This Gopālaka-mantra is indeed the swift giver of the fruits desired.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Keśava presents the Gopāla mantra as efficacious across ordinary social and ritual boundaries.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is representative because the Krama-Dīpikā is not only a theology of Kṛṣṇa-worship but also a manual about who may receive and use the mantra. Keśava is defining the practical social reach of Gopāla-upāsanā here, so the verse captures a real doctrinal commitment of the text rather than an anthologized ornament.
key_passages.source_edition: Sudhakar Malaviya (ed.), Keśavakāśmīri: Kramadīpikā with the commentary by Govindabhaṭṭācārya, Benares: Krishnadas Academy, 1989 (Krishnadas Sanskrit Series 119), via GRETIL e-text.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
nūnam acyuta-kaṭākṣa-pātena
kāraṇaṃ bhavati bhaktir añjasā |
tac-catuṣṭaya-phalāptaye tato
bhaktimān adhikṛto harau gurau ||

pada_analysis.gloss: by the fall of Acyuta's glance
for the attainment of that fourfold result

samasa_vigrahas.note: The commentary identifies the fourfold result as the puruṣārtha-catuṣṭaya.

karaka_structure.note: instrumental cause of bhakti
subject of bhavati
purpose/end expressed by the dative
subject of the implied predication with adhikṛtaḥ
sphere of devotional qualification
paired sphere of devotional qualification

key_passages.english_close: Certainly, by the descent of Acyuta's sidelong glance. Bhakti directly becomes the cause [of the fruit]. Therefore, for attaining that fourfold result. One possessed of bhakti is qualified with respect to Hari and the guru.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Keśava says that divine grace gives rise to bhakti, and that bhakti is what authorizes the practitioner for disciplined relation to Hari and guru.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: devotional participation directed to the Lord)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is programmatic for the manual: grace initiates bhakti, and bhakti grounds ritual and spiritual eligibility before both Hari and guru. That sequence recurs throughout the text, so this is a fair statement of Keśava's operative soteriology rather than a detached devotional flourish.
key_passages.source_edition: Sudhakar Malaviya (ed.), Keśavakāśmīri: Kramadīpikā with the commentary by Govindabhaṭṭācārya, Benares: Krishnadas Academy, 1989 (Krishnadas Sanskrit Series 119), via GRETIL e-text.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
athavā gopījana iti samasta-jagad-vana-śakti-samudāyaḥ |
tasya svānanyasya svāmī vallabha iti ha nirdiṣṭaḥ ||
key_passages.text_critical_note: The printed/GRETIL line reads `jagad-vana-śakti-samudāyaḥ`; Govindabhaṭṭācārya's commentary glosses the phrase as `sakala-viśva-rakṣaṇa-śakti-samudāyaḥ`, which fixes the intended sense as the aggregate of the powers that protect the whole world.

pada_analysis.gloss: the aggregate of the powers that protect the whole world
of what is non-other than himself

samasa_vigrahas.note: Here the expression is being reinterpreted symbolically within the verse itself.
The commentary adds `svābhinnasya`, making the non-otherness explicit.

karaka_structure.note: genitive relation: Vallabha is the lord of that power-aggregate, which is non-other than himself
subject of the implied predication with nirdiṣṭaḥ

key_passages.english_close: Or else, `gopījana` is the aggregate of the powers that protect the whole world; of that, which is non-other than himself, Vallabha is indeed indicated as the Lord.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Keśava reads `gopījana` as the totality of divine world-sustaining powers and Kṛṣṇa as their Lord, without severing those powers from him.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: non-otherness, not being separate from its ground)

key_passages.why_this_passage: Because only the Kramadīpikā is available on disk in this run, explicit metaphysical lines like this matter disproportionately: here Keśava states, rather than merely presupposes, a Nimbārka-style non-separateness of divine power and its lord. That makes the verse a fair witness to his bhedābheda theology within a ritual text.
key_passages.source_edition: Sudhakar Malaviya (ed.), Keśavakāśmīri: Kramadīpikā with the commentary by Govindabhaṭṭācārya, Benares: Krishnadas Academy, 1989 (Krishnadas Sanskrit Series 119), via GRETIL e-text.

key_passages.locus_long: Kramadīpikā 6.5, mantroddhāra of the aṣṭādaśākṣara Mukunda-mantra

pada_analysis.gloss: the two feet of Śrī Mukunda

samasa_vigrahas.note: The honorific `śrī` is carried within the proper-name compound.

karaka_structure.note: subject of prapadye
the object to which the speaker resorts
predicate accusative: the mode in which the feet are approached

key_passages.english_close: I always resort, as refuge, to the two feet of Śrī Mukunda.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: The mantra is explicitly framed as self-surrender at Mukunda's feet.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is representative because the work does not treat mantra as bare phonetic formula: its explicit shape is surrender at Mukunda's feet. The line therefore captures the prapatti-current that runs through Keśava's manual and gives the surrounding ritual its theological orientation.
key_passages.source_edition: Sudhakar Malaviya (ed.), Keśavakāśmīri: Kramadīpikā with the commentary by Govindabhaṭṭācārya, Benares: Krishnadas Academy, 1989 (Krishnadas Sanskrit Series 119), via GRETIL e-text.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
manum imam atihṛdyaṃ yo bhajed bhakti-namro
japa-huta-yajanādyair dhyānavān mantri-mukhyaḥ |
truṭita-sakala-karma-granthir udbuddha-cetāḥ
vrajati sa tu padaṃ tan nitya-śuddhaṃ murāreḥ ||

pada_analysis.gloss: with recitation, oblation, worship, and the like
one whose knot of all karma is broken

karaka_structure.note: subject of both bhajed and vrajati
object of bhajed
means by which the mantra is honored
goal attained through vrajati
genitive relation to padam

key_passages.english_close: Whoever, made bhakti-bowed, would honor this exceedingly delightful mantra. By means of recitation, oblation, worship, and the rest — with meditation. With the knot of all karma severed, his consciousness fully awakened. He, the foremost mantra-practitioner, indeed goes to that eternally-pure station of Murāri.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Keśava's mature promise is that devotionally enacted mantra-practice cuts karmic bondage and leads the practitioner to Murāri's pure abode.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: the binding knot formed by karmic entanglement)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a fair capstone because it states the manual's mature promise in one place: mantra-bhajana, performed with devotion and meditation, breaks karmic bondage and leads to Murāri's pure state. Given that only the Kramadīpikā is available on disk for this run, this verse is especially useful as a concise witness to Keśava's soteriological endpoint.
key_passages.source_edition: Sudhakar Malaviya (ed.), Keśavakāśmīri: Kramadīpikā with the commentary by Govindabhaṭṭācārya, Benares: Krishnadas Academy, 1989 (Krishnadas Sanskrit Series 119), via GRETIL e-text.
